title: Emergent mirror symmetry in the optimization of the central-spin quantum battery
authors: Hui-Yu Yang, Kun Zhang, Xiao-Hui Wang, Hai-Long Shi
abstract: Quantum batteries provide a useful setting for exploring nonequilibrium many-body effects in energy storage. Here we investigate the optimization of a quantum battery based on the central-spin model. We identify two complementary structural indicators associated with the effective charging dynamics: one yields an upper bound on the average charging power, while the other characterizes the buildup of stored energy. We show that these two indicators are jointly optimized at a distinguished initial charger excitation number, which selects a particular Dicke sector of the model. At this common optimal point, the effective charging Hamiltonian becomes exactly mirror symmetric, suggesting mirror symmetry as a useful structural indicator for optimizing quantum batteries. We further show that the corresponding optimal dynamics can be closely approximated by product initial states, in particular by spin coherent states whose excitation-number distribution is centered at the symmetry-selected point. Our results establish a direct connection between charging performance, optimal-state structure, and emergent symmetry in the central-spin quantum battery, and suggest symmetry as a useful organizing principle for efficient charging in interacting many-body quantum systems.
summary: This paper investigates the optimization of energy storage in a central-spin quantum battery model. By analyzing two structural indicators related to charging dynamics, the authors find that maximum efficiency is achieved when the system's effective Hamiltonian becomes exactly mirror symmetric. This establishes emergent symmetry as a key organizing principle for maximizing performance in interacting quantum systems.
main_problem: The research aims to optimize the charging efficiency of a quantum battery by identifying structural indicators that govern energy storage dynamics.
main_result: Joint optimization of two structural indicators ($\Omega(n)$ and $\Lambda(n)$) occurs at an initial charger excitation number ($n^*$) where the effective charging Hamiltonian exhibits exact mirror symmetry. This emergent symmetry is shown to be a useful principle for maximizing charging performance.
method: The authors derived upper bounds on average charging power and stored energy using structural indicators related to hopping profiles, followed by optimization techniques (AM-GM inequality) to find the optimal initial state.
model_or_system: The system is a central-spin quantum battery model consisting of $N_b$ central spins coupled to $N_c$ bath spins. The dynamics are analyzed within an invariant subspace defined by the charger's Dicke state sector, governed by a specific interaction Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Average charging power ($ ar{P}(	au)$), stored energy ($E(	au)$), and two structural indicators: $\Omega(n)$ (upper bound on power) and $\Lambda(n)$ (stored energy buildup characterization).
important_parameters: The optimal excitation number $n^*$, which is found to be near $(N_b + N_c)/2$. The analysis also considers the large $N_c$ limit for scaling laws.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on bounding techniques (Hölder, AM-GM) and approximates the optimal Dicke state dynamics using product initial states like spin coherent states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate that both stored energy and average power are maximized at $n^*$, confirming the physical relevance of the symmetry selection. Figure 4 specifically shows the scaling of the optimal charging time ($	au \propto 1/N_c$).
paper_structure: The paper first defines the quantum battery model and its associated structural indicators. It then mathematically derives upper bounds for performance metrics using these indicators. The core is the optimization step, showing that maximizing both indicators simultaneously selects $n^*$, which in turn enforces mirror symmetry on the effective Hamiltonian.
why_interesting: This work connects abstract mathematical concepts (emergent symmetry) directly to measurable physical quantities (charging power/energy), providing a powerful theoretical tool for designing and understanding optimal operation regimes in complex quantum many-body systems relevant to AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Time evolution of the normalized stored-energy contribution associated with the fully charged battery state, ε(t) = ⟨Nb|ρ(t)|Nb⟩, for different initial charger excitation numbers n. At the optimal point n = n∗, the transfer to the fully charged state is the most efﬁcient, leading to the largest and fastest buildup of ε(t). Here n∗= (Nb+Nc)/2 for the even case considered. The parameters are Nb = 5, Nc = 25, and A = 1.

FIG. 1. Spatial proﬁle of the effective hopping amplitudes uj for different initial charger excitation numbers n. At the optimal point n = n∗, the proﬁle is exactly mirror symmetric with respect to the chain center, whereas away from this point it becomes asymmetric. Here n∗= (Nb + Nc)/2 for the even case considered. The parame- ters are Nb = 30, Nc = 90, and A = 1.

FIG. 4. Scaling of the charging time τ with the charger size Nc. Red circles denote the numerical results for the symmetry-selected opti- mal protocol n = n∗, blue circles denote those for the fully excited charger baseline n = Nc, and the red solid line is the 1/Nc scaling guide. The optimal protocol follows τ ∝1/Nc, whereas the fully excited charger baseline does not. Here n∗= (Nb + Nc)/2 for the even case considered. The parameters are Nb = 5 and A = 1.

FIG. 3. Charging performance as a function of the initial charger excitation number n. The maximum stored energy per battery cell E(τ)/Nb in (a) and the corresponding average charging power ¯P(τ) in (b) are both maximized at the common optimal point n = n∗, marked by the vertical dashed line. Here n∗= (Nb + Nc)/2 for the even case considered. The parameters are Nb = 5, Nc = 25, and A = 1.

Figure 4 conﬁrms this prediction by comparing the symmetry-selected protocol with the fully polarized bench- mark. The red circles, corresponding to the optimal Dicke- state protocol at n = n∗, are in excellent agreement with the 1/Nc reference scaling marked by the red solid line. In contrast, the blue circles, corresponding to the fully polar- ized charger scheme n = Nc, decrease much more slowly and clearly deviate from the 1/Nc behavior. This compari- son demonstrates that the reduction of the charging time is not a trivial consequence of enlarging the charger, but origi- nates from the structure of the symmetry-selected optimal ini- tial state.
